agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"An existing logistics/courier system uses 'confirmed' as a well-defined order status meaning paid and queued for fulfillment. A new feature request proposes reusing the term 'confirmed' for quotes, where it would mean something fundamentally different: verbal agreement with no payment. This creates a direct collision in the ubiquitous language.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Quoting — these may be separate bounded contexts, but the term 'confirmed' is being proposed for use across both with contradictory semantics.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed (Order)","definition":"Existing definition: An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable."},{"term":"Confirmed (Quote — proposed)","definition":"Proposed definition: A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT yet occurred. Mutability is unspecified."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A pre-order pricing agreement presented to a customer by a sales agent."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A commitment to fulfill a delivery/service, progressing through statuses including 'confirmed'."},{"term":"Immutable","definition":"Once an entity reaches this state, its core attributes (line items, pricing, addresses) cannot be changed."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"The term 'confirmed' on an Order means payment has been received and the order is queued for fulfillment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an established domain definition encoding a state-integrity guarantee. Downstream systems (warehouse, dispatch, billing) depend on 'confirmed' meaning 'paid'. Changing this meaning corrupts the fulfillment pipeline.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in 'confirmed' status, THEN payment has been received AND the order is queued for fulfillment."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A confirmed order is immutable — no modifications to its core data are permitted after confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Immutability after confirmation prevents state corruption in downstream fulfillment, billing, and audit systems.","testable_assertion":"Given a confirmed order, WHEN any update to line items, pricing, or addresses is attempted, THEN the system rejects the update."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A quote's 'confirmed' status (as proposed) means verbal agreement only — no payment has occurred.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a proposed business policy for a new workflow step. It represents a soft commitment, not a financial or fulfillment-integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given a quote in 'confirmed' status, THEN no payment record exists for that quote."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The ubiquitous language must use unambiguous terms — the same term must not carry different meanings across closely related entities.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Ambiguous domain language causes misinterpretation by developers, support staff, reporting systems, and integrations. In logistics, acting on the wrong meaning of 'confirmed' could trigger premature fulfillment or missed SLAs.","testable_assertion":"Given any status term used in the system, THEN it has exactly one semantic definition regardless of which entity it applies to."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"The existing system with 'confirmed' defined as paid-and-queued for orders","when":"A quote is moved to a status indicating verbal customer agreement","then":"The status term used for the quote MUST be distinct from the order's 'confirmed' status to prevent semantic collision"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms","when":"The sales agent records this agreement","then":"The quote transitions to a status that clearly communicates 'agreed but unpaid' (e.g., 'accepted', 'customer_accepted', or 'verbally_agreed')"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A confirmed order (existing behavior)","when":"Any system component queries 'confirmed' entities","then":"The query unambiguously returns only paid, fulfillment-queued orders — never unpaid quotes"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A quote in the new verbal-agreement status","when":"The quote is referenced in reports, APIs, or downstream systems","then":"No system interprets the quote as having been paid or as being ready for fulfillment"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A reporting query filters all 'confirmed' entities across orders and quotes","expected_behavior":"Without disambiguation, unpaid quotes would appear alongside paid orders, corrupting fulfillment dashboards, revenue reports, and SLA tracking."},{"scenario":"An integration partner receives a webhook for a 'confirmed' entity","expected_behavior":"The partner's system would assume payment occurred and may trigger shipping, inventory allocation, or invoicing prematurely."},{"scenario":"A sales agent tells a customer their quote is 'confirmed' and the customer assumes it means paid/processing","expected_behavior":"Customer-facing language must distinguish between verbal agreement and paid-and-processing to avoid fulfillment expectation mismatches."},{"scenario":"A quote is 'confirmed' (verbal) and the customer later disputes they agreed","expected_behavior":"Verbal agreement status has no payment backing; the system must not treat this as a binding financial commitment without additional verification."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. If 'confirmed' is reused, systems may prematurely recognize revenue, trigger fulfillment costs, or allocate inventory against unpaid quotes. Misreported revenue could have compliance implications.","operational":"Severe. Fulfillment pipelines, warehouse management, and dispatch systems that key on 'confirmed' would conflate unpaid quotes with paid orders, causing erroneous picks, shipments, and SLA tracking failures.","user":"Sales agents and operations staff would lose trust in the status system. Customer-facing communications using 'confirmed' would be ambiguous, leading to support escalations.","risk":"Domain language corruption is one of the highest-risk categories in DDD. It cascades silently — systems built today work, but every future developer, integration, and report inherits the ambiguity. This is technical debt that compounds.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Need a clear status for verbal agreement; reusing 'confirmed' creates confusion about whether payment occurred."},{"group":"Warehouse / Fulfillment","impact":"Risk of premature picking and shipping if 'confirmed' quotes are mistaken for 'confirmed' orders."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Revenue recognition errors if 'confirmed' quotes are counted as paid commitments."},{"group":"Integration Partners","impact":"API consumers relying on 'confirmed' semantics would misinterpret quote statuses."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Ambiguous confirmation language could set incorrect expectations about order processing."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: 'confirmed' is explicitly defined as paid-and-immutable for orders, and the feature request explicitly proposes 'confirmed' for quotes meaning unpaid-verbal-agreement. These two definitions cannot coexist without corrupting the ubiquitous language. Confidence is low because shipping this as-specified would violate an established domain invariant.","high_confidence_areas":["The semantic collision between the two uses of 'confirmed' is unambiguous","The business need for a verbal-agreement status on quotes is legitimate","The existing 'confirmed' order invariant (paid + immutable) is well-established"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Quote and Order live in truly separate bounded contexts with isolated language","What the full quote lifecycle looks like (other statuses, transitions)"],"assumptions":["Orders and Quotes are visible in shared dashboards, APIs, and reporting — not fully isolated bounded contexts","Downstream systems and integrations key on the string value 'confirmed' for triggering fulfillment actions"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' has an established, invariant definition for orders (paid, immutable, queued for fulfillment). The feature request proposes reusing 'confirmed' for quotes with a contradictory definition (verbal agreement, unpaid, mutability unspecified). These two meanings directly conflict and cannot coexist in the ubiquitous language without causing domain corruption.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Warehouse / Fulfillment","Finance / Billing","Integration Partners","Customers"],"options":["A) Rename the quote status to 'accepted' or 'customer_accepted' to preserve the existing meaning of 'confirmed'","B) Rename the quote status to 'verbally_agreed' to make the no-payment semantics explicit","C) If Quote truly lives in a separate bounded context with no shared reporting or APIs, allow 'confirmed' with explicit anti-corruption layer — but verify isolation first"],"recommendation":"Option A: Use 'accepted' for the quote status. It accurately conveys customer agreement without implying payment, preserves the established meaning of 'confirmed', and requires zero changes to existing order logic. This is the lowest-risk path that satisfies the business need."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Reusing 'confirmed' for quotes with a different meaning than its established definition on orders","business_rule_broken":"BR-4: Ubiquitous language must be unambiguous — the same term must not carry different meanings across closely related entities. Also threatens BR-1: downstream systems depending on 'confirmed' meaning 'paid' could misprocess unpaid quotes."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"An existing logistics/courier system uses 'confirmed' as a well-defined order status meaning paid and queued for fulfillment. A new feature request proposes reusing the term 'confirmed' for quotes, where it would mean something fundamentally different: verbal agreement with no payment. This creates a direct collision in the ubiquitous language.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Quoting — these may be separate bounded contexts, but the term 'confirmed'

content.thinking:
The scenario presents a clear contradiction: the term "confirmed" is already established in the domain language for orders (meaning paid and queued for fulfillment), and now a new feature request wants to use "confirmed" for quotes with a completely different meaning (verbal agreement, no payment). This is a domain language collision.

This is a contradiction - two explicit definitions for the same term in the same domain.

These two meanings directly conflict and cannot coexist in the ubiquitous language without causing domain corruption.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Warehouse / Fulfillment","Finance / Billing","Integration Partners","Customers"],"options":["A) Rename the quote status to 'accepted' or 'customer_accepted' to preserve the existing meaning of 'confirmed'","B) Rename the quote status to 'verbally_agreed' to make the no-payment semantics explicit","C) If Quote truly lives in a separate bounded context with no shared reporting or APIs, allow 'confirmed' with explicit anti-corruption layer — but verify isolation first"],"recommendation":"Option A: Use 'accepted' for the quote status.
